text: The surgeon continues dissection in the retrosternal space, gradually expanding the view. Occasionally, a portion of the diaphragm is cut to increase the surgical field. Careful deflection from the middle is used to avoid tearing small blood vessels.
The surgeon opens the right pleural cavity and places a 5mm additional port in the fifth intercostal space, providing a clear view of the right phrenic nerve and enhancing operability.
The surgeon dissects the right epiphramic fat pads from the diaphragm and pericardium. After visually confirming the left phrenic nerve, the resection line is determined on the caudal side.
The surgeon lifts the fat and thymic tissue off the pericardium, exposing the junction of the right and left brachiocephalic veins.

The surgeon pulls the thymus tissue towards the caudal side and dissects the right superior horn and surrounding fat tissue using a light gasser V held in the left hand. Forceps and a Maryland dissector are utilized through the additional port to achieve an ideal dissection angle.
The surgeon seals the mid-veins using a ligature Maryland device. The left superior horn is identified and divided safely, utilizing both right and left approaches for optimal access and precision.

The surgeon obtains a clear view of the right brachiocephalic artery, trachea, left common carotid artery, and right brachiocephalic vein. The thymectomy specimen is placed in a plastic bag and removed through the sub-xiphoid incision.
